relevant_files.annotation: CLI binary entry point. Defines the root `fabric` command using citty, registers all subcommands via `allCommands`, and invokes `runMain` when executed directly. This is the top of the entire interaction chain.

Central command registry for the CLI. Exports `allCommands` map that lazy-imports all subcommands: init, scan, serve, doctor, sync-meta, human-lint, ledger-append, pre-commit, bootstrap, config, hooks. Defines the full surface area of the CLI.

CLI->Server bridge. The `serve` command parses port/host/auth args, resolves dev-mode target directory, reads FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN from env, and calls `startHttpServer()` from fabric-server. This is the entry point that boots the entire HTTP+MCP server stack.

One-shot project initialization command. Creates AGENTS.md, .fabric/agents.meta.json, human-lock.json, forensic.json; copies Claude skill+hook templates; merges Claude Stop hooks into .claude/settings.json; runs bootstrap+MCP install+hooks stages. Establishes all data files the server runtime depends on.

MCP client auto-detection logic. `resolveClients()` detects Claude Code CLI/Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, RooCode, Gemini CLI, and Codex by probing filesystem markers (~/.claude, .cursor/, etc.) and instantiates the appropriate writer for each. Determines which AI hosts receive MCP server configuration.

JSON-based MCP config writers for Claude Code CLI (project .claude/settings.json), Claude Code Desktop, Cursor (.cursor/mcp.json), Windsurf, RooCode, and Gemini CLI. `writeJsonClientConfig()` merges the `fabric` entry into mcpServers. Defines how the MCP Host learns to connect to the server.

Claude Code Desktop-specific writer. Computes OS-specific claude_desktop_config.json paths (macOS Library/Application Support, Windows AppData, Linux ~/.config). `writeClaudeCodeAll()` installs MCP config for both CLI and Desktop variants.

MCP Server entry point and factory. `createFabricServer()` creates an McpServer instance and registers all four MCP tools. `startStdioServer()` connects via StdioServerTransport for CLI-embedded use; `startHttpServer()` boots the full HTTP app. Dual-transport design is the core architectural choice.

HTTP app factory and MCP session management. `createFabricHttpApp()` mounts all REST API routes, creates the SSE events handler, sets up StreamableHTTPServerTransport for MCP-over-HTTP, and implements `JsonlEventStore` for MCP event persistence/replay. Session map (`Map<string, FabricHttpSession>`) manages per-connection McpServer+transport pairs. The MCP endpoint at /mcp handles initialize+subsequent requests.

MCP tool: `fab_append_intent`. Called MANDATORY by the agent after completing any task. Delegates to `appendIntent` service. The tool description instructs the agent on when to call it, making this a key point in the agent->MCP loop.

MCP tool: `fab_get_rules`. Called MANDATORY by the agent before modifying any file. Accepts `path` and optional `client_hash` for stale detection. Returns L0/L1/L2 layered rules plus human_locked_nearby. This is the primary context delivery mechanism from server to agent.

MCP tool: `fab_plan_context`. Batch variant of get-rules for planning/architecture phases. Accepts multiple paths in one call, deduplicates, returns per-path rule payloads. Reduces round-trips during multi-file planning.

MCP tool: `fab_update_registry`. MANDATORY for add/remove/update of registry nodes instead of direct file editing. Performs atomic write to agents.meta.json and recomputes revision_hash. Ensures meta stays consistent under agent mutations.

`getRules()` service: loads agents.meta.json synchronously, reads AGENTS.md, checks human-lock state, matches target path against scope_glob patterns using minimatch, sorts matched nodes by priority (high/medium/low), loads rule file contents, appends audit event. Implements the L0/L1/L2 rules resolution algorithm.

`planContext()` service: loads context once then resolves rules for all unique paths in parallel via `Promise.all`. Shares `GetRulesContext` across all path lookups to avoid redundant meta/AGENTS.md reads — key performance optimization for multi-path queries.

`appendIntent()` service: assigns timestamp, appends entry to .intent-ledger.jsonl, then best-effort calls `appendEditIntentAuditEvents` (failure does not block). Returns `{success, timestamp, entry}`.

`updateRegistry()` service: reads current agents.meta.json, applies add/remove/update operation, recomputes SHA256 revision from sorted node hashes, atomically writes new meta. Ensures revision stays deterministic and consistent.

Compliance audit log subsystem. `appendGetRulesAuditEvent()` records every tool call with path+client_hash. `appendEditIntentAuditEvents()` cross-checks each affected_path against preceding get_rules events within a 5-minute window to set `compliant: boolean`. Provides compliance trail without blocking tool responses.

History replay service. `rehydrateAgentsMetaAt()` walks the ledger to a target entry, attempts git-show to recover agents.meta.json from commit SHAs (prefer newest commit first), falls back to reconstructing meta from affected_paths. Powers the `/api/history/state` endpoint for dashboard time-travel.

`readAgentsMeta()` reads and parses .fabric/agents.meta.json synchronously (readFileSync). `resolveProjectRoot()` reads from FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT env (set by HTTP app) or process.cwd(). This synchronous read is on every tool call hot path.

SSE push subsystem. `createEventsHandler()` manages up to 10 concurrent SSE clients with 30s heartbeat. Uses chokidar to watch agents.meta.json, human-lock.json, forensic.json, and .intent-ledger.jsonl. File changes trigger debounced (75ms) event publishing to all clients. Implements incremental ledger tail-reading using file offsets. This is the real-time channel from server to Web Client.

REST API for human-lock entries: GET /api/human-lock (list), GET /api/human-lock/:file (single), POST /api/human-lock/approve. Guards all endpoints with readAgentsMeta check for Fabric presence. Connects dashboard Approve button to approve-human-lock service.

REST API POST /api/intent/annotate. Validates body against annotateIntentRequestSchema, calls annotateIntent service to append a human annotation ledger entry. Connects dashboard annotation UI to ledger.
Optional Bearer token auth middleware applied to /api and /events routes when FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN is set. Allows remote/public hosting of the dashboard without exposing API to unauthenticated clients.

Web Client HTTP+SSE client layer. Exports typed functions: getRules, getLedger, getScan, getDoctor, getHumanLock, approveHumanLock, annotateIntent, getHistoryState, getEvents (EventSource), parseFabricEvent. All REST calls use fetch with Accept: application/json. SSE via browser-native EventSource. The complete API surface the dashboard uses to communicate with the server.

React-style hook for SSE event management. Opens EventSource on mount, maps typed event listeners for all 5 FabricEvent types, accumulates last 50 events in signal, tracks connected/disconnected state. Signals (`@preact/signals-core`) allow cross-component reactive updates without prop drilling.

Dashboard root. Defines 5 routes (rules/locks/timeline/history/doctor) via hash-based routing. Mounts `useEvents()` at app root and passes `lastEvent` prop to each view for event-driven refresh. Shows SSE connection status badge.

Shared FabricEvent discriminated union: meta:updated (AgentsMeta), lock:drift, lock:approved, ledger:appended (LedgerEntry), drift:detected (ForensicReport). Both server (broadcast) and dashboard (parse) use this schema, ensuring type-safe SSE payloads across the boundary.

Shared LedgerEntry discriminated union schema: aiLedgerEntrySchema (source:ai, optional commit_sha) and humanLedgerEntrySchema (source:human, parent_sha, diff_stat, annotation). Pre-processes missing source field as 'human'. Used by MCP tool input validation, ledger read/write, and SSE event payloads.

AgentsMeta schema: `{revision: string, nodes: Record<string, AgentsMetaNode>}` where each node has file, scope_glob, deps, priority, layer (L0/L1/L2), topology_type (mirror/cross-cutting), hash. `deriveAgentsMetaLayer()` and `deriveAgentsMetaTopologyType()` auto-classify nodes from file paths. Defines the core data contract for the rules registry.

Shared Zod schemas for REST API request validation: ledgerQuerySchema, historyStateQuerySchema (exactly one of ledger_id or ts), humanLockApproveRequestSchema, annotateIntentRequestSchema. Used by both server-side route handlers and dashboard client type definitions.
Core type definitions: AgentsLayer (L0/L1/L2), AgentsTopologyType (mirror/cross-cutting), AgentsMetaNode, AgentsMeta. The shared type contract consumed by CLI, server, and dashboard.

patterns: Dual-transport MCP server: StdioServerTransport (for CLI-embedded/direct-launch) and StreamableHTTPServerTransport (for web-accessible serve mode)
Four MCP tools form the full agent loop: fab_get_rules (before-edit gate) -> fab_plan_context (planning batch gate) -> fab_append_intent (post-task recorder) -> fab_update_registry (meta mutation)

FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT environment variable as implicit context injection: set by startHttpServer, read by resolveProjectRoot() on every tool call, eliminating projectRoot parameter threading through MCP handlers
Synchronous readFileSync for agents.meta.json in meta-reader.ts (hot path on every tool call) — potential contention under concurrent requests
JsonlEventStore persists MCP streaming events to .intent-ledger.jsonl for replay/reconnection support (EventStore interface from @modelcontextprotocol/sdk)
SSE event system separate from MCP transport: chokidar watches 4 key files, 75ms debounce, broadcasts typed FabricEvent to <=10 concurrent browser clients
Compliance audit trail: every fab_get_rules call is logged; every fab_append_intent cross-checks for preceding get_rules event within 5-minute window, sets compliant:boolean
L0/L1/L2 rules layering: L0=AGENTS.md global, L1=cross-cutting or depth<=2, L2=deep module rules. Agent receives applicable layer content filtered by scope_glob minimatch
Atomic file writes via temp-file+rename pattern (atomicWriteText) for agents.meta.json to prevent partial-read corruption
Session-per-connection McpServer instances: each HTTP /mcp initialize creates a new McpServer + StreamableHTTPServerTransport pair, stored in sessions Map
Multi-client MCP configuration: resolveClients() auto-detects 7 AI host types (Claude CLI, Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, RooCode, Gemini CLI, Codex) by filesystem markers
Hash-based revision tracking: agents.meta.json carries a revision SHA256 hash computed from all node hashes; clients can send client_hash to detect stale rules
History time-travel: rehydrateAgentsMetaAt() first tries git-show to recover past agents.meta.json state, falls back to reconstructing meta from ledger affected_paths

Human-lock drift detection on SSE: events.ts compares current file hash (SHA256 of locked line range) against stored hash on every human-lock.json change, emits lock:drift or lock:approved accordingly

@fenglimg/fabric-cli.description: User-facing CLI binary (fab/fabric). Commands: init (one-shot project setup, MCP config install), serve (boots HTTP+MCP server), scan (framework detection), doctor (health checks), sync-meta (meta sync), human-lint, ledger-append, pre-commit. Acts as MCP Host configuration installer and as the launcher for the MCP server.

@fenglimg/fabric-server.description: The MCP server exposing 4 tools (fab_get_rules, fab_plan_context, fab_append_intent, fab_update_registry) to AI agents. Also serves a REST HTTP API for the dashboard (/api/*) and SSE event stream (/events). Supports both stdio transport (direct/embedded) and HTTP Streamable transport (serve mode). Manages project state: agents.meta.json, ledger, human-lock, forensic.

@fenglimg/fabric-dashboard.description: Preact SPA served as static files from the HTTP server. 5 views: rules-tree, human-lock, intent-timeline, history-replay, doctor. Communicates with server via REST API (fetch) and real-time SSE (/events). Dashboard is embedded in server dist at build time via scripts/copy-dashboard.mjs.

@fenglimg/fabric-shared.description: Shared types, Zod schemas, and i18n used across CLI, server, and dashboard. Key contracts: AgentsMeta/AgentsMetaNode types, LedgerEntry discriminated union, FabricEvent union (SSE), API request schemas (api-contracts), HumanLockEntry, FabricConfig. The single source of truth for data shapes crossing component boundaries.

questions_for_user: The `readAgentsMeta()` in meta-reader.ts uses synchronous `readFileSync` on the hot path for every MCP tool call. Under concurrent agent requests (multiple tool calls in parallel), is this a known bottleneck or intentional for simplicity?

Each HTTP /mcp initialize creates a new `McpServer` instance (createFabricServer) with its own tool registrations. Is a pool or singleton server design preferred to reduce initialization overhead for frequent agent sessions?
The SSE connection limit is hardcoded to 10 concurrent clients (CONNECTION_LIMIT). Is this limit intentional (single-user local tool assumption) or should it be configurable for team/CI scenarios?

The audit compliance check (5-minute window matching get_rules to append_intent) reads the entire audit.jsonl on every fab_append_intent call. Will this scale if the audit log grows large, or is periodic truncation/rotation planned?

fab_plan_context uses `Promise.all` for parallel rule resolution but fab_get_rules resolves a single path. Are there scenarios where an agent should prefer fab_get_rules over fab_plan_context for single-file edits, or should fab_plan_context replace fab_get_rules entirely?

The dashboard is embedded in the server package at build time via copy-dashboard.mjs. Is there a desire to support external/hot-reload dashboard development mode that bypasses the embedded static files?

The CLI serve command does not launch the stdio MCP server — it only starts HTTP. Is stdio transport exclusively used when the MCP Host launches the server binary directly (e.g., Claude Desktop mcpServers config), while serve mode is for dashboard access only?
The `fab_update_registry` tool allows agents to mutate the rules registry directly. Is there a plan for access control or approval gate (similar to human-lock) before registry mutations take effect?
